title: Coordinate Plane
blurb: Plot and read points in all four quadrants, reflect across the axes, measure distance, and build polygons on the grid.

lessons.title: The Coordinate Plane
lessons.concreteComparison: A theatre ticket says “Row D, Seat 7” — you need **two** numbers to find one seat. The coordinate plane works the same way: an **across** number and an **up** number, written as an ordered pair $(x, y)$.
lessons.objective: Identify the axes, origin, and quadrants of the coordinate plane and read an ordered pair (x, y).
concept: The **coordinate plane** is made of two number lines: the horizontal **x-axis** and the vertical **y-axis**.
A point is named by an **ordered pair** $(x, y)$: $x$ is how far **across**, $y$ is how far **up**.
The axes split the plane into four **quadrants**, numbered I–IV counter-clockwise from the top-right.
lessons.keyIdea: A point is $(x, y)$ — across, then up. The axes meet at the origin $(0,0)$ and form four quadrants.

facts.title: The origin
facts.explanation: Where the x-axis and y-axis cross.

workedExamples.solution: Both coordinates are positive, so Quadrant I.
Both are negative, so Quadrant III.

commonMistakes.correction: The order is $(x, y)$: across **first**, then up. $(3,2) \neq (2,3)$.
The origin $(0,0)$ sits on both axes — it's in no quadrant.

steps.title: Four quadrants
steps.teach: The two axes split the plane into four regions called **quadrants**, numbered I–IV counter-clockwise from the top-right. The sign of each coordinate tells you which one you're in.

steps.title: Tap to read coordinates
steps.teach: Tap any intersection. Count **across** from the origin for $x$, then **up** for $y$ — that's the point's ordered pair.

steps.title: Which quadrant?
steps.intro: Check the signs of x and y.

examples.prompt: A point has a negative $x$ and negative $y$. Which quadrant?

steps.title: On an axis?
steps.intro: Zero means on an axis, not in a quadrant.

problems.explanation: $x = 0$, so it's on the vertical y-axis.

steps.title: Descartes and the fly
steps.body: Legend says René Descartes invented this grid while lying in bed watching a fly on the ceiling. He realized he could pin down the fly's spot with just two numbers — its distance from two walls. That idea, the coordinate plane, connected algebra and geometry forever.

steps.title: What you learned
points: The plane has an **x-axis** (across) and **y-axis** (up) meeting at the **origin** $(0,0)$.
A point is $(x, y)$: across first, then up.
Four **quadrants**, I–IV counter-clockwise; signs tell you which.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review the plane with quadrant IDs, a move-from-the-origin question, and an on-the-axis check.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: From the origin, move 4 right and 3 down. Where do you land?

steps.title: You've finished "The Coordinate Plane"
steps.body: Great start — you know the axes, the origin, and all four quadrants. Next: Plotting Points, where you'll place and read points anywhere on the grid.

lessons.title: Plotting Points
lessons.concreteComparison: A treasure map clue: “$3$ steps east, $2$ steps north.” **Plotting a point** is the same move — start at the origin, step across for $x$, then up for $y$, and mark the spot.
lessons.objective: Plot points from ordered pairs and read the coordinates of plotted points in all four quadrants.
concept: To **plot** $(x, y)$: start at the origin, go $x$ **across** (right if $+$, left if $-$), then $y$ **up** (up if $+$, down if $-$).
To **read** a point: count across for $x$, then up for $y$.
A negative $x$ goes left; a negative $y$ goes down.
lessons.keyIdea: Plot $(x,y)$ by stepping $x$ across from the origin, then $y$ up or down. Read a point the same way.

workedExamples.statement: Plot $(3, 2)$: describe the move from the origin.
workedExamples.solution: Go $3$ right, then $2$ up — landing in Quadrant I.

workedExamples.statement: A point is $2$ left and $4$ up from the origin. Name it.

commonMistakes.correction: Always across first, then up: $(3, 2)$ means $3$ across, $2$ up.
A negative $x$ means go **left**; a negative $y$ means go **down**.

tryIt.statement: Which point is $5$ right and $0$ up?

steps.title: A plotted point
steps.teach: This point is $(3, 2)$: from the origin, $3$ across and $2$ up. The pair is always written across-then-up.

steps.title: Start at the origin
beats: Step **across** by $x$: right for $+$, left for $-$.
Then step **up** by $y$: up for $+$, down for $-$.

steps.title: Read points yourself
steps.teach: Tap intersections and read off the coordinates. Notice how moving left makes $x$ negative and moving down makes $y$ negative.

problems.prompt: Start at the origin: $2$ right, $3$ up. Which point?
Which point is $1$ left and $4$ down?

steps.title: Left and down
beats: A negative $x$ sends you **left** of the y-axis.
A negative $y$ sends you **down** below the x-axis.

examples.prompt: A point sits $4$ left and $2$ up. Name it.

problems.prompt: $3$ right and $5$ down is which point?

steps.title: You've plotted before
steps.body: Every game of Battleship is coordinate plotting — “B-5” is an ordered pair in disguise. So is every pixel on this screen: the phone draws each dot at its own $(x, y)$ address, millions of times a second.

steps.title: Plot it
steps.prompt: From the origin, go $2$ left and $3$ up. Which point?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Plot** $(x, y)$: from the origin, $x$ across then $y$ up.
**Read** a point by counting across then up — order matters.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review plotting with move-and-land questions, a reading check, and a same-point puzzle.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: From the origin, go $3$ right and $4$ up. Where?
Which point is $2$ left and $5$ up?
Which point is $4$ right and $1$ down?
Going $6$ down from the origin lands on which point?
Do $(2, 5)$ and $(5, 2)$ name the same point?
problems.explanation: Order matters — $2$ across, $5$ up is a different spot from $5$ across, $2$ up.

steps.title: You've finished "Plotting Points"
steps.body: Nicely done — you can plot and read points across all four quadrants. Next: Reflections, where points hop across an axis to their mirror image.

lessons.title: Reflections Across the Axes
lessons.concreteComparison: Your reflection in a mirror stands the same distance behind the glass as you are in front. Reflecting a point across an axis does exactly that — same distance, opposite side — which flips the sign of one coordinate.
lessons.objective: Reflect a point across the x-axis or y-axis and describe how its coordinates change.
concept: Reflecting across the **x-axis** flips up/down: $(x, y) \to (x, -y)$.
Reflecting across the **y-axis** flips left/right: $(x, y) \to (-x, y)$.
The image is the same distance from the axis, on the opposite side.
lessons.keyIdea: Reflect across the x-axis → negate $y$. Reflect across the y-axis → negate $x$.

commonMistakes.correction: Across the x-axis, **only** $y$ changes sign; across the y-axis, **only** $x$ does.
The x-axis is horizontal, so it flips the up/down value ($y$). The y-axis flips the left/right value ($x$).

steps.title: Flip across the x-axis
steps.teach: The point is $(3, 4)$. Tap **Reflect across the x-axis** — its image lands at $(3, -4)$, the same distance below the axis. Only the $y$-coordinate flips.

steps.title: Negate the y
beats: The x-axis is the horizontal mirror.

steps.title: Negate the x
beats: The y-axis is the vertical mirror.

steps.title: Flip across the y-axis
steps.teach: Now the mirror is the y-axis. Tap to reflect $(2, 3)$ — it lands at $(-2, 3)$, the same distance to the left. Only the $x$-coordinate flips.

steps.title: Same distance, opposite side
beats: Same distance, other side of the mirror.

steps.title: Across which axis?
steps.intro: Match the flip to the axis.

steps.title: Nature loves a mirror
steps.body: Butterflies, faces, and snowflakes are nearly symmetric — one half is the reflection of the other across a line. Designers and animators use axis reflections constantly, drawing one side of a shape and letting the computer flip it to make the rest.

steps.title: What you learned
points: The image is the **same distance** from the axis, on the opposite side.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review reflections across both axes, including points that already start negative.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Reflecting across the x-axis changes the sign of which coordinate?
problems.explanation: The x-axis is horizontal, so the up/down value $y$ flips.

steps.title: You've finished "Reflections Across the Axes"
steps.body: Excellent — you can flip a point across either axis and know exactly which coordinate changes. Next: Distance, where we measure how far apart two points are.

lessons.title: Distance on the Coordinate Plane
lessons.concreteComparison: Two friends live on the same street. To find how far apart they are, you just count the blocks between them. On the grid, two points on the same horizontal or vertical line are a countable number of units apart — the difference of the coordinates that change.
lessons.objective: Find the distance between two points that share an x-coordinate or a y-coordinate.
concept: Points on the same **horizontal line** (same $y$) are $|x_1 - x_2|$ apart.
Points on the same **vertical line** (same $x$) are $|y_1 - y_2|$ apart.
Use **absolute value** so distance is never negative; across the origin, the parts add up.
lessons.keyIdea: Same row → subtract the $x$'s; same column → subtract the $y$'s. Take the absolute value.

facts.title: Same column
facts.explanation: When the x's match, distance is the y-difference.

commonMistakes.text: Subtracting the coordinates that are the same.
commonMistakes.correction: If the $y$'s match, the points differ in $x$ — subtract the **x's**.
Distance is $|\text{difference}|$ — always zero or positive.

steps.title: Count the units
steps.teach: These two points share the same $x$, so they sit on a vertical line. The distance is just the difference of the $y$'s: $|7 - 3| = 4$.

steps.title: Across the origin
steps.teach: These points share $y = 1$ but sit on opposite sides of the y-axis. The distance still works: $|5 - (-3)| = 8$.

steps.title: Use absolute value
beats: Sometimes points are on opposite sides of an axis.
Subtracting a negative adds — that's the full width.

steps.title: Taxicab distance
steps.body: This “count along the lines” distance is called **taxicab** or **Manhattan** distance — a cab in a grid city can't cut diagonally through buildings, so it travels the difference in streets plus the difference in avenues. Whole branches of math and computer science use it.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Use **absolute value**; across an axis the parts add.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review distance along rows and columns, including a pair that spans the origin.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Points $(3, 2)$ and $(3, 9)$ share the same $x$. What do you subtract?

steps.title: You've finished "Distance on the Coordinate Plane"
steps.body: Great — you can measure horizontal and vertical distances, even across an axis. Last in this unit: Polygons, where these side lengths build shapes.

lessons.title: Polygons on the Coordinate Plane
lessons.concreteComparison: Laying out a rectangular garden, you hammer a stake at each corner. Given the corners' coordinates, you can find each side's length by counting units — and add them up for the fence you'll need (the perimeter).
lessons.objective: Find side lengths and the perimeter of a rectangle drawn on the coordinate plane from its vertices.
concept: A polygon's **vertices** are ordered pairs; plot them and connect in order.
A **horizontal side** is $|x_1 - x_2|$ long; a **vertical side** is $|y_1 - y_2|$ long.
The **perimeter** is the sum of all the side lengths.
lessons.keyIdea: Side length = difference of the coordinate that changes. Perimeter = add up every side.

workedExamples.statement: A rectangle has corners $(1,1), (5,1), (5,4), (1,4)$. How long is the bottom side from $(1,1)$ to $(5,1)$?

commonMistakes.text: Adding only two sides for perimeter.
commonMistakes.correction: A rectangle has **four** sides; perimeter $= 2\ell + 2w$, not $\ell + w$.

commonMistakes.text: Using the wrong coordinate for a side.
commonMistakes.correction: For a horizontal side the $y$'s match, so measure with the $x$'s.

tryIt.statement: Length of the side from $(2,3)$ to $(2,8)$?
Perimeter of a square whose side runs from $(0,0)$ to $(4,0)$?

steps.title: A rectangle from its corners
steps.teach: These four vertices connect into a rectangle. The bottom runs from $(1,1)$ to $(5,1)$ — a horizontal side of length $|5-1| = 4$. The left side runs $(1,1)$ to $(1,4)$ — length $3$.

steps.title: Sides from coordinates
beats: Plot the vertices and connect them in order.
Each side length is a whole number of units.

steps.title: Perimeter of a rectangle
beats: Find the length and the width.

examples.prompt: Rectangle with length $5$ and width $3$: perimeter?

steps.title: Coordinates build everything
steps.body: Every shape in a video game or animated film is stored as a list of vertex coordinates. The computer connects them into polygons and fills them in — a spaceship or a whole city is, underneath, just ordered pairs joined in the right order.

steps.title: Whole way around
steps.prompt: A rectangle has length $6$ and width $3$. What is its perimeter?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Vertices** are ordered pairs; connect them in order to draw the shape.
**Side length** = difference of the coordinate that changes.
**Perimeter** = sum of all sides; for a rectangle, $2\ell + 2w$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review with side-length measures and a couple of full-rectangle perimeters.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Length of the side from $(2,1)$ to $(2,7)$?
Length of the side from $(-2,3)$ to $(4,3)$?
A rectangle has length $7$ and width $2$. Perimeter?
A rectangle has corners $(1,1),(6,1),(6,3),(1,3)$. What is its length (bottom side)?
A square has a side from $(0,0)$ to $(0,5)$. What is its perimeter?

steps.title: You've finished "Polygons on the Coordinate Plane"
steps.body: Fantastic — you can build shapes from vertices and find their side lengths and perimeter. That completes the Coordinate Plane!
